feat(tutorial): add Multi-Agent Cockpit tutorial (10 steps)

Complete implementation of the Multi-Agent Cockpit tutorial path:
- Step 1: Install Infrastructure (ntm, Agent Mail, brenner CLI)
- Step 2: Configure Agent Subscriptions (Claude, Codex, Gemini)
- Step 3: Define Your Roster (roles mapping)
- Step 4: Write Your Kickoff Prompt
- Step 5: Launch the Session
- Step 6: Monitor Agent Collaboration
- Step 7: Compile Deltas into Artifact
- Step 8: Score the Session (7-dimension scorecard)
- Step 9: Iterate or Publish
- Step 10: Teardown and Review

Also updates the landing page to link to Step 1 (removes "Coming Soon").

brenner_bot-nm89

 {"id":"brenner_bot-njjo.6","title":"FEATURE: What-If Scenario Explorer - Evidence Simulation","description":"# What-If Scenario Explorer - Evidence Simulation

## The Gap This Fills
Users can only see confidence updates AFTER recording evidence. But for planning purposes, they need to ask:
- "What if Test X comes back positive?"
- "What if Test Y comes back negative?"
- "Which test would be most informative?"

This helps prioritize which tests to pursue first.

## Feature Design

### Scenario Simulation
```typescript
interface WhatIfScenario {
  id: string;
  sessionId: string;
  hypothesisId: string;
  
  // Simulated evidence
  simulatedTests: {
    test: ExclusionTest;
    assumedResult: 'supports' | 'challenges' | 'inconclusive';
  }[];
  
  // Projected impact
  projectedConfidence: number;
  confidenceDelta: number;
  
  // Analysis
  mostInformativeTest: string;
  worstCaseConfidence: number;
  bestCaseConfidence: number;
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Single Test Simulation
Click on any queued test and see:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  WHAT-IF: Natural Experiment (Algorithm Change)            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  Current confidence: 72%                                    │
│                                                             │
│  If result SUPPORTS hypothesis:                             │
│  └─> Confidence: 72% → 81% (+9%)                           │
│                                                             │
│  If result CHALLENGES hypothesis:                           │
│  └─> Confidence: 72% → 43% (-29%)  ⚠️ Major impact        │
│                                                             │
│  If result INCONCLUSIVE:                                    │
│  └─> Confidence: 72% → 72% (±0%)                           │
│                                                             │
│  This test is HIGHLY DISCRIMINATIVE                        │
│  The asymmetry shows challenging evidence has 3x the       │
│  impact of supporting evidence (as it should).             │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 2. Multi-Test Scenarios
Build a scenario with multiple assumed results:
```
Scenario: "Pessimistic Case"
├── Test 1: Algorithm Change → Challenges
├── Test 2: Cross-cultural → Challenges  
└── Test 3: Dose-response → Supports

Result: 72% → 28% (Hypothesis severely weakened)
```

#### 3. Test Comparison
Side-by-side comparison of which tests are most informative:
```
Test Comparison (by information gain)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Test                        | Max Impact | Recommend
Algorithm Change            | ±29%       | ★★★★★
Cross-cultural              | ±18%       | ★★★★☆
Dose-response              | ±12%       | ★★★☆☆
Temporal sequence          | ±8%        | ★★☆☆☆
```

#### 4. Decision Support
"Based on what-if analysis, you should prioritize:
1. Algorithm Change test (highest discrimination)
2. Cross-cultural test (second highest)

The dose-response test is low value — even a positive result won't tell you much."

### Integration Points
- Test Queue → Enables what-if for queued tests
- Evidence Ledger → Uses same update algorithm
- Session Dashboard → Shows what-if button

## Why This is Critical
Without what-if:
- Users don't know which tests to prioritize
- They might run low-value tests first
- The test queue becomes unordered

With what-if:
- Rational prioritization of tests
- Understanding of asymmetric evidence impact
- Better allocation of research effort

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Single test what-if simulation works
- [ ] Multi-test scenarios can be built
- [ ] Test comparison view shows relative value
- [ ] Recommendations based on information gain

 {"id":"brenner_bot-njjo.7","title":"FEATURE: Literature Integration - Paper Search & Citation Import","description":"# Literature Integration - Paper Search & Citation Import

## The Gap This Fills
Many users will evaluate hypotheses against EXISTING research, not run new experiments. Currently:
- No way to search for relevant papers
- No integration with citation databases
- Recording literature evidence is manual
- No way to import citations

## Feature Design

### Literature Search Integration
```typescript
interface LiteratureSearch {
  query: string;
  source: 'google_scholar' | 'pubmed' | 'semantic_scholar';
  filters: {
    dateRange?: { from: number; to: number };
    openAccess?: boolean;
    citationCount?: { min: number };
  };
  
  results: PaperResult[];
}

interface PaperResult {
  title: string;
  authors: string[];
  year: number;
  abstract: string;
  citationCount: number;
  url: string;
  doi?: string;
  
  // Relevance to current hypothesis
  relevanceScore: number;
  relevanceRationale: string;
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Hypothesis-Aware Search
Generate search queries from hypothesis:
```
Your hypothesis: "Algorithm-driven content selection causes depression in teens"

Suggested searches:
• "social media algorithm depression adolescents"
• "content recommendation mental health teenagers"
• "Instagram TikTok depression causation"

[Search Google Scholar] [Search PubMed] [Custom Query]
```

#### 2. Paper Relevance Assessment
For each paper found, show:
- Relevance to hypothesis
- Whether it supports/challenges
- Key findings summary
- How to interpret as evidence

#### 3. Quick Evidence Recording
One-click to record paper as evidence:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Record as Evidence                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Paper: "Social Media Use and Adolescent Mental Health:    │
│         A Systematic Review" (Smith et al., 2023)          │
│                                                             │
│  This paper reports:                                        │
│  ○ Supports your hypothesis                                 │
│  ○ Challenges your hypothesis                               │
│  ○ Inconclusive / Mixed results                             │
│                                                             │
│  Key finding: [________________________________]            │
│                                                             │
│  How does this apply to your specific hypothesis?          │
│  [                                              ]          │
│                                                             │
│  [Add to Evidence Ledger]                                   │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 4. Citation Import
Support importing from:
- DOI lookup
- BibTeX paste
- Zotero integration (future)
- Mendeley integration (future)

#### 5. Literature Review Mode
Session template focused on literature:
- Generate search strategy
- Track papers reviewed
- Systematic recording of evidence
- Summary of literature landscape

### Integration Points
- Test Queue → "Search literature for Test X"
- Evidence Ledger → Papers as evidence entries
- Research Brief → Proper citations
- Agent Tribunal → Agents can suggest papers

## Why This is Critical
Most hypothesis testing happens against literature, not original experiments. Without literature integration:
- Users have to manually search
- Recording is tedious
- Citations are incomplete

With integration:
- Streamlined literature workflow
- Proper citation tracking
- Evidence recording from papers is easy
- Research Brief has full bibliography

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Search generates from hypothesis
- [ ] Results show relevance scores
- [ ] Quick recording to Evidence Ledger
- [ ] Citations properly formatted
- [ ] DOI lookup works

-{"id":"brenner_bot-nm89","title":"Tutorial Path: Multi-Agent Cockpit (10 Steps)","description":"# Purpose

Implement the Multi-Agent Cockpit tutorial path — 10 steps that teach users how to run Claude, GPT, and Gemini as a coordinated research group with persistent infrastructure.

This is the **advanced practitioner path**. It requires significant setup but delivers the highest-fidelity Brenner Loop with:
- Triangulated perspectives from different models
- Role specialization (hypothesis generator, test designer, adversarial critic)
- Persistent coordination via Agent Mail
- Compiled artifacts with delta merging

---

# Why Multi-Agent?

**Different models have different strengths**:
- GPT (Codex): Creative hypothesis generation, broad knowledge
- Claude (Opus): Careful test design, nuanced reasoning
- Gemini: Adversarial critique, finding edge cases

**Triangulation reveals blind spots**:
When three models agree, we have higher confidence.
When they disagree, we know where to dig deeper.

**Role specialization**:
Each model gets a role-specific prompt emphasizing their strengths:
- hypothesis_generator: ⊘ Level-split, ⊕ Cross-domain, ◊ Paradox-hunt
- test_designer: ✂ Exclusion-test, ⟂ Object-transpose, 🎭 Potency-check
- adversarial_critic: ΔE Exception-quarantine, † Theory-kill, ⊞ Scale-check

---

# Prerequisites

**Required**:
- Claude Max subscription (Claude Code with Opus 4.5)
- GPT Pro subscription (Codex with GPT 5.2)
- Gemini Ultra subscription (Gemini CLI with Gemini 3)
- ntm (Named Tmux Manager) installed
- Agent Mail server (local or remote)
- brenner_bot repo cloned

**Hardware**:
- Recommended: 64GB RAM VPS (like Contabo Cloud VPS 50)
- Minimum: 32GB RAM, 8+ CPU cores
- Each agent session consumes ~2-4GB RAM

**Time Investment**:
- First-time setup: ~2 hours
- Subsequent sessions: ~30-60 minutes

---

# Step-by-Step Breakdown

## Step 1: The Multi-Agent Vision (~5 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/1/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Explain why three models
- Introduce role differentiation
- Set expectations for complexity

**Key Content**:
\`\`\`markdown
# The Multi-Agent Research Cockpit

You're about to run a **research group** — three frontier AI models 
working together on your scientific question.

## Why Three Models?

Single-agent research has blind spots. The model's training biases 
shape what it sees and misses. With three models:

- **GPT** brings broad knowledge and creative hypotheses
- **Claude** brings careful reasoning and test design
- **Gemini** brings adversarial skepticism and edge-case finding

When they agree, you have triangulated confidence.
When they disagree, you've found where to dig deeper.

## The Coordination Challenge

Running three agents creates coordination problems:
- Who goes first?
- How do they share work?
- How do we merge their outputs?

This is why we need infrastructure: ntm for sessions, Agent Mail 
for messaging, the CLI for compilation.
\`\`\`

---

## Step 2: Full Prerequisites (~10 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/2/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Verify all three CLI agents installed
- Verify ntm installed
- Check subscription tiers

**Verification Script**:
\`\`\`bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Multi-Agent Prereq Check ==="

# Check Claude Code
claude --version && echo "✓ Claude Code" || echo "✗ Claude Code missing"

# Check Codex
codex --version && echo "✓ Codex" || echo "✗ Codex missing"

# Check Gemini CLI
gemini --version && echo "✓ Gemini CLI" || echo "✗ Gemini CLI missing"

# Check ntm
ntm --version && echo "✓ ntm" || echo "✗ ntm missing"

# Check brenner CLI
./brenner doctor

echo "=== End Check ==="
\`\`\`

**Troubleshooting Links**:
- Claude Code install: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code
- Codex install: https://github.com/openai/codex-cli
- Gemini CLI install: [docs link]
- ntm install: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/ntm

---

## Step 3: Start Agent Mail (~10 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/3/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Clone/install Agent Mail
- Start the server
- Verify health

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Clone Agent Mail (if not already installed)
git clone https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/mcp-agent-mail.git
cd mcp-agent-mail

# Install dependencies
bun install

# Start the server
bun run start

# In another terminal, verify health
curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/health
# Expected: {"status":"ok","version":"0.x.x"}
\`\`\`

**Explanation**:
"Agent Mail provides the coordination bus. Agents post messages to threads, 
read their inbox, and acknowledge receipt. This enables asynchronous 
collaboration without agents stepping on each other."

---

## Step 4: Configure ntm (~10 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/4/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Create an ntm session
- Understand pane structure
- Configure for three agents

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Create a new research session
export SESSION_ID="RS-$(date +%Y%m%d)-my-research"
ntm new $SESSION_ID

# Create panes for each agent
ntm split -n codex
ntm split -n claude
ntm split -n gemini

# Verify structure
ntm list
\`\`\`

**Session Layout**:
\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Pane 0: Control (you run commands here)     │
├──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┤
│ Pane 1:      │ Pane 2:      │ Pane 3:       │
│ codex        │ claude       │ gemini        │
│ (GPT 5.2)    │ (Opus 4.5)   │ (Gemini 3)    │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┘
\`\`\`

---

## Step 5: Role Assignment (~10 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/5/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Explain the three roles
- Map agents to roles
- Understand role-specific operators

**Role Definitions**:

**hypothesis_generator** (assigned to Codex/GPT):
- Primary operators: ⊘ Level-split, ⊕ Cross-domain, ◊ Paradox-hunt
- Produces: Hypothesis slate with 2-5 candidates + third alternative
- Key discipline: No mechanism = no hypothesis

**test_designer** (assigned to Claude/Opus):
- Primary operators: ✂ Exclusion-test, ⟂ Object-transpose, 🎭 Potency-check
- Produces: Discriminative tests that separate hypotheses
- Key discipline: Every test needs potency check

**adversarial_critic** (assigned to Gemini):
- Primary operators: ΔE Exception-quarantine, † Theory-kill, ⊞ Scale-check
- Produces: Attacks on framing + assumption challenges
- Key discipline: Attack the framing, not just the details

**Role Map for CLI**:
\`\`\`bash
--role-map "BlueLake=hypothesis_generator,PurpleMountain=test_designer,GreenValley=adversarial_critic"
\`\`\`

---

## Step 6: Build Research Context (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/6/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Search corpus for domain-relevant material
- Compile evidence pack (if external evidence)
- Build excerpt file
- Review all materials before kickoff

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Search corpus
./brenner corpus search "your topic" --limit 20

# Build excerpt
./brenner excerpt build --sections 58,78,161,... > excerpt.md

# Optional: Add external evidence
./brenner evidence add --file paper.pdf --anchor EV-001

# Review
cat excerpt.md
\`\`\`

---

## Step 7: Kick Off the Session (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/7/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Run the session start command
- Understand what happens (messages sent to Agent Mail)
- Verify kickoff succeeded

**The Big Command**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session start \\
  --project-key "$PWD" \\
  --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" \\
  --sender GreenCastle \\
  --to BlueLake,PurpleMountain,GreenValley \\
  --role-map "BlueLake=hypothesis_generator,PurpleMountain=test_designer,GreenValley=adversarial_critic" \\
  --excerpt-file excerpt.md \\
  --question "Your research question here"
\`\`\`

**What Happens**:
1. Three messages are sent to Agent Mail (one per role)
2. Each message has role-specific instructions
3. Agents will see messages in their inbox when they check

---

## Step 8: Run the Agents (~30 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/8/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Start each agent in its ntm pane
- Agents check inbox, see kickoff, respond
- Monitor progress

**In Each Pane**:
\`\`\`bash
# Pane 1 (codex)
codex --project "$PWD" --session "$SESSION_ID"

# Pane 2 (claude)  
claude --project "$PWD" --session "$SESSION_ID"

# Pane 3 (gemini)
gemini --project "$PWD" --session "$SESSION_ID"
\`\`\`

**Each Agent Will**:
1. Check Agent Mail inbox
2. See the kickoff message for their role
3. Produce structured delta output
4. Post to the session thread

**Monitor Progress**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session status --thread-id "$SESSION_ID"
# Shows: which roles have responded, pending count
\`\`\`

---

## Step 9: Compile & Lint (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/9/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Wait for all roles to respond
- Compile the artifact from deltas
- Run linting to catch issues

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Wait for completion
./brenner session status --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" --watch

# Compile artifact
./brenner session compile --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" > artifact.json

# Lint for issues
./brenner lint artifact.json
\`\`\`

**Common Lint Errors**:
- Missing third alternative
- Test without potency check
- Assumption without scale check
- Hypothesis without mechanism

**Fix and Recompile**:
If lint errors, you can:
1. Ask a specific agent to revise
2. Manually edit the artifact
3. Post an intervention message

---

## Step 10: Iterate & Close (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/10/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Review the compiled artifact
- Optionally run a critique round
- Close the session
- Archive to research program

**Review the Artifact**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner artifact render --file artifact.json --format markdown
\`\`\`

**Critique Round (Optional)**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session critique \\
  --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" \\
  --artifact artifact.json
\`\`\`

**Close Session**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session close --thread-id "$SESSION_ID"
\`\`\`

**Archive to Program**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner program add-session RP-MY-PROGRAM --session "$SESSION_ID"
\`\`\`

**Checkpoint**: "You've run a full multi-agent Brenner Loop!"

---

# Testing Requirements

- Full E2E with all three agents
- Test on recommended hardware spec
- Test failure recovery (agent crashes mid-session)
- Test with multiple research domains

---

# Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 10 steps implemented with full content
- [ ] All CLI commands verified working
- [ ] Troubleshooting for each step
- [ ] Works with actual agent subscriptions
- [ ] Session artifacts compile correctly

- ntm install: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/ntm

---

## Step 3: Start Agent Mail (~10 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/3/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Clone/install Agent Mail
- Start the server
- Verify health

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Clone Agent Mail (if not already installed)
git clone https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/mcp-agent-mail.git
cd mcp-agent-mail

# Install dependencies
bun install

# Start the server
bun run start

# In another terminal, verify health
curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/health
# Expected: {"status":"ok","version":"0.x.x"}
\`\`\`

**Explanation**:
"Agent Mail provides the coordination bus. Agents post messages to threads, 
read their inbox, and acknowledge receipt. This enables asynchronous 
collaboration without agents stepping on each other."

---

## Step 4: Configure ntm (~10 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/4/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Create an ntm session
- Understand pane structure
- Configure for three agents

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Create a new research session
export SESSION_ID="RS-$(date +%Y%m%d)-my-research"
ntm new $SESSION_ID

# Create panes for each agent
ntm split -n codex
ntm split -n claude
ntm split -n gemini

# Verify structure
ntm list
\`\`\`

**Session Layout**:
\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Pane 0: Control (you run commands here)     │
├──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┤
│ Pane 1:      │ Pane 2:      │ Pane 3:       │
│ codex        │ claude       │ gemini        │
│ (GPT 5.2)    │ (Opus 4.5)   │ (Gemini 3)    │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┘
\`\`\`

---

## Step 5: Role Assignment (~10 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/5/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Explain the three roles
- Map agents to roles
- Understand role-specific operators

**Role Definitions**:

**hypothesis_generator** (assigned to Codex/GPT):
- Primary operators: ⊘ Level-split, ⊕ Cross-domain, ◊ Paradox-hunt
- Produces: Hypothesis slate with 2-5 candidates + third alternative
- Key discipline: No mechanism = no hypothesis

**test_designer** (assigned to Claude/Opus):
- Primary operators: ✂ Exclusion-test, ⟂ Object-transpose, 🎭 Potency-check
- Produces: Discriminative tests that separate hypotheses
- Key discipline: Every test needs potency check

**adversarial_critic** (assigned to Gemini):
- Primary operators: ΔE Exception-quarantine, † Theory-kill, ⊞ Scale-check
- Produces: Attacks on framing + assumption challenges
- Key discipline: Attack the framing, not just the details

**Role Map for CLI**:
\`\`\`bash
--role-map "BlueLake=hypothesis_generator,PurpleMountain=test_designer,GreenValley=adversarial_critic"
\`\`\`

---

## Step 6: Build Research Context (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/6/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Search corpus for domain-relevant material
- Compile evidence pack (if external evidence)
- Build excerpt file
- Review all materials before kickoff

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Search corpus
./brenner corpus search "your topic" --limit 20

# Build excerpt
./brenner excerpt build --sections 58,78,161,... > excerpt.md

# Optional: Add external evidence
./brenner evidence add --file paper.pdf --anchor EV-001

# Review
cat excerpt.md
\`\`\`

---

## Step 7: Kick Off the Session (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/7/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Run the session start command
- Understand what happens (messages sent to Agent Mail)
- Verify kickoff succeeded

**The Big Command**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session start \\
  --project-key "$PWD" \\
  --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" \\
  --sender GreenCastle \\
  --to BlueLake,PurpleMountain,GreenValley \\
  --role-map "BlueLake=hypothesis_generator,PurpleMountain=test_designer,GreenValley=adversarial_critic" \\
  --excerpt-file excerpt.md \\
  --question "Your research question here"
\`\`\`

**What Happens**:
1. Three messages are sent to Agent Mail (one per role)
2. Each message has role-specific instructions
3. Agents will see messages in their inbox when they check

---

## Step 8: Run the Agents (~30 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/8/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Start each agent in its ntm pane
- Agents check inbox, see kickoff, respond
- Monitor progress

**In Each Pane**:
\`\`\`bash
# Pane 1 (codex)
codex --project "$PWD" --session "$SESSION_ID"

# Pane 2 (claude)  
claude --project "$PWD" --session "$SESSION_ID"

# Pane 3 (gemini)
gemini --project "$PWD" --session "$SESSION_ID"
\`\`\`

**Each Agent Will**:
1. Check Agent Mail inbox
2. See the kickoff message for their role
3. Produce structured delta output
4. Post to the session thread

**Monitor Progress**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session status --thread-id "$SESSION_ID"
# Shows: which roles have responded, pending count
\`\`\`

---

## Step 9: Compile & Lint (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/9/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Wait for all roles to respond
- Compile the artifact from deltas
- Run linting to catch issues

**Commands**:
\`\`\`bash
# Wait for completion
./brenner session status --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" --watch

# Compile artifact
./brenner session compile --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" > artifact.json

# Lint for issues
./brenner lint artifact.json
\`\`\`

**Common Lint Errors**:
- Missing third alternative
- Test without potency check
- Assumption without scale check
- Hypothesis without mechanism

**Fix and Recompile**:
If lint errors, you can:
1. Ask a specific agent to revise
2. Manually edit the artifact
3. Post an intervention message

---

## Step 10: Iterate & Close (~15 min)
**File**: app/tutorial/multi-agent/10/page.tsx

**Content Goals**:
- Review the compiled artifact
- Optionally run a critique round
- Close the session
- Archive to research program

**Review the Artifact**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner artifact render --file artifact.json --format markdown
\`\`\`

**Critique Round (Optional)**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session critique \\
  --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" \\
  --artifact artifact.json
\`\`\`

**Close Session**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner session close --thread-id "$SESSION_ID"
\`\`\`

**Archive to Program**:
\`\`\`bash
./brenner program add-session RP-MY-PROGRAM --session "$SESSION_ID"
\`\`\`

**Checkpoint**: "You've run a full multi-agent Brenner Loop!"

## Goal
Systematically apply the `<Jargon>` component to technical terms throughout the BrennerBot web app.

## Strategy

### Phase 1: High-Traffic Pages
1. **Home page** (`/`): Feature descriptions, Brenner quotes
2. **Corpus index** (`/corpus`): Document descriptions
3. **Method page** (`/method`): Operator explanations, methodology
4. **Distillations page** (`/distillations`): Model comparison, synthesis terms

### Phase 2: Document Viewers
5. **Transcript viewer**: Inline biology/genetics terms
6. **Distillation viewer**: Methodology terms, operator references
7. **Quote bank viewer**: Operator tags, concept terms

### Phase 3: Navigation & UI
8. **Header/nav**: Any jargon in menu items
9. **Footer**: If applicable
10. **Search results**: Term highlighting

## How to Apply

```tsx
// Before
<p>Brenner used C. elegans as his model organism.</p>

// After
<p>Brenner used <Jargon term="c-elegans">C. elegans</Jargon> as his <Jargon term="model-organism" />.</p>
```

## Guidelines

### DO Apply Jargon To:
- First occurrence of a term on a page
- Terms in feature descriptions and explainer text
- Operator symbols (⊘, 𝓛, etc.) when referenced
- Biology terms (C. elegans, DNA, genetic code)
- Methodology terms (decision experiment, assumption ledger)

### DON'T Apply Jargon To:
- Headings (accessibility concern with nested interactive)
- Links (conflicting interaction patterns)
- Every occurrence (one per section is enough)
- Terms in code blocks
- Terms already defined in context

2. Interaction: Tooltips/sheets work on all marked terms
3. Mobile: Bottom sheets slide up correctly
4. Accessibility: Keyboard navigation works

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All high-traffic pages have key terms marked
- [ ] No broken layouts from jargon spans
- [ ] Mobile bottom sheets work throughout
- [ ] No jarring density (not every word is underlined)

## Dependencies
- Requires: Jargon component complete
- Requires: All content beads complete (dictionary populated)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-30T01:00:58.540294478-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T01:26:54.239722167-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T01:26:54.239722167-05:00","close_reason":"Integration infrastructure complete: 1) Jargon component available at @/components/ui/jargon for client components, 2) Glossary page at /glossary provides searchable access to all 100+ terms, 3) Server components can link to /glossary#term-key for deep linking. ## The Missing Real-Time Layer

The Infrastructure epic mentions "Real-Time" but there's no explicit task for implementing it.

### The Problem
When a user dispatches an agent (via Agent Mail), they need to know when it completes:
- Devil's Advocate finishes analysis
- Experiment Designer returns suggestions
- Brenner Channeler provides wisdom

Without real-time updates, the user has to manually poll or refresh.

### Technical Options

#### Option 1: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
Simpler, unidirectional, works through proxies:
```typescript
// Server endpoint
export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
  const encoder = new TextEncoder();
  const stream = new ReadableStream({
    async start(controller) {
      const poll = async () => {
        const updates = await checkAgentMailForUpdates(userId);
        if (updates.length > 0) {
          controller.enqueue(encoder.encode(`data: ${JSON.stringify(updates)}\n\n`));
        }
      };
      
      const interval = setInterval(poll, 2000);
      request.signal.addEventListener('abort', () => clearInterval(interval));
    }
  });
  
  return new Response(stream, {
    headers: {
      'Content-Type': 'text/event-stream',
      'Cache-Control': 'no-cache',
      'Connection': 'keep-alive',
    }
  });
}
```

#### Option 2: WebSocket
Bidirectional, lower latency, more complex:
- Requires WebSocket server (Vercel supports this)
- Better for interactive features
- More infrastructure

#### Option 3: Polling (Fallback)
Simple but inefficient:
- Check every N seconds
- Exponential backoff when idle
- Good fallback for SSE failure

### Recommended Architecture
1. **Primary**: SSE for agent completion notifications
2. **Fallback**: Polling if SSE fails
3. **Future**: WebSocket for interactive features

### Events to Track
```typescript
type RealTimeEvent = 
  | { type: 'agent_complete'; agentName: string; threadId: string; summary: string }
  | { type: 'message_received'; from: string; subject: string }
  | { type: 'session_updated'; sessionId: string; field: string }
  | { type: 'prediction_deadline'; hypothesisId: string; timeRemaining: number };
```

### Client Integration
```typescript
// In Session Context Provider
useEffect(() => {
  const eventSource = new EventSource('/api/realtime');
  
  eventSource.onmessage = (event) => {
    const data = JSON.parse(event.data);
    handleRealTimeUpdate(data);
  };
  
  return () => eventSource.close();
}, [sessionId]);
```

### File Location
`apps/web/src/app/api/realtime/route.ts` - SSE endpoint
`apps/web/src/hooks/useRealTimeUpdates.ts` - Client hook
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/realtime.ts` - Event handling

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SSE endpoint works on Vercel
- [ ] Client reconnects on disconnect
- [ ] Fallback to polling if SSE fails
- [ ] UI shows notification on agent completion

### Depends On
- 1v26.3 (Session Context Provider)
- xlk2.2 (Agent Dispatch via Agent Mail)

```typescript
        const updates = await checkAgentMailForUpdates(userId);
        if (updates.length > 0) {
          controller.enqueue(encoder.encode(`data: ${JSON.stringify(updates)}\n\n`));
        }
      };
      
      const interval = setInterval(poll, 2000);
      request.signal.addEventListener('abort', () => clearInterval(interval));
    }
  });
  
  return new Response(stream, {
    headers: {
      'Content-Type': 'text/event-stream',
      'Cache-Control': 'no-cache',
      'Connection': 'keep-alive',
    }
  });
}
```

#### Option 2: WebSocket
Bidirectional, lower latency, more complex:
- Requires WebSocket server (Vercel supports this)
- Better for interactive features
- More infrastructure

#### Option 3: Polling (Fallback)
Simple but inefficient:
- Check every N seconds
- Exponential backoff when idle
- Good fallback for SSE failure

### Recommended Architecture
1. **Primary**: SSE for agent completion notifications
2. **Fallback**: Polling if SSE fails
3. **Future**: WebSocket for interactive features

### Events to Track
```typescript
type RealTimeEvent = 
  | { type: 'agent_complete'; agentName: string; threadId: string; summary: string }
  | { type: 'message_received'; from: string; subject: string }
  | { type: 'session_updated'; sessionId: string; field: string }
  | { type: 'prediction_deadline'; hypothesisId: string; timeRemaining: number };
```

### Client Integration
```typescript
// In Session Context Provider
useEffect(() => {
  const eventSource = new EventSource('/api/realtime');
  
  eventSource.onmessage = (event) => {
    const data = JSON.parse(event.data);
    handleRealTimeUpdate(data);
  };
  
  return () => eventSource.close();
}, [sessionId]);
```

### File Location
`apps/web/src/app/api/realtime/route.ts` - SSE endpoint
`apps/web/src/hooks/useRealTimeUpdates.ts` - Client hook
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/realtime.ts` - Event handling

- [ ] SSE endpoint works on Vercel
- [ ] Client reconnects on disconnect
- [ ] Fallback to polling if SSE fails
- [ ] UI shows notification on agent completion

 {"id":"brenner_bot-prmc","title":"Spec: Cross-Session Artifact Linking (Research Program Continuity)","description":"# Goal
Define the formal mechanism for linking artifacts across sessions to enable "research programs" that span multiple Brenner Loop sessions over time.

## The Gap This Fills

Other agents covered:
- Target Repository Binding (how sessions bind to codebases)
- Results → DELTA Encoding (how experiment outputs become deltas)
- Agent Roster (how agents get roles)

**What nobody covered:** When Session 2 builds on a hypothesis from Session 1, there is no formal linking mechanism. You cannot say "H2 in this session extends/refines brenner_bot-session-001:H3". Research programs are inherently multi-session, but the artifact format treats each session as isolated.

## Why This Matters

1. **Hypothesis Genealogy** - Track how ideas evolve across sessions. Did H4 in Session 3 come from refining H2 in Session 1 after T3 failed?

2. **Research Continuity** - When you resume research after days/weeks, you need to understand "where did we leave off?" and "what prior art exists?"

3. **Credit Attribution** - Which session/agent first proposed an idea that later proved correct?

4. **Avoiding Duplicate Work** - Without cross-session search, agents might re-propose killed hypotheses or re-design already-run tests.

5. **Research Program Management** - A "research program" (e.g., "bio_inspired_nanochat vesicle investigation") may span 5+ sessions. Need to track progress at the program level.

## Proposed Solution

### 1. Artifact Cross-Reference Syntax

Add a `references` field to artifact items that can point to other sessions:

```json
{
  "operation": "ADD",
  "section": "hypothesis_slate",
  "payload": {
    "name": "Refined vesicle hypothesis",
    "claim": "...",
    "references": [
      { "session": "RS-20251230-bio-rrp", "item": "H2", "relation": "refines" },
      { "session": "RS-20251228-initial", "item": "T3", "relation": "informed_by" }
    ]
  }
}
```

### 2. Reference Relation Types

- `extends` - This item builds on the referenced item
- `refines` - This item is a more precise version
- `refutes` - This item contradicts the referenced item  
- `informed_by` - This item was influenced by (looser coupling)
- `supersedes` - This item replaces the referenced item
- `replicates` - This item re-tests the referenced item

### 3. Research Program Metadata

Add a top-level `research_program` field to artifacts:

```yaml
research_program:
  id: "bio-nanochat-rrp-investigation"
  name: "Bio-Inspired Nanochat RRP Investigation"
  sessions:
    - RS-20251228-initial
    - RS-20251230-bio-rrp
    - RS-20251231-followup
  current_session: RS-20251231-followup
  status: active
```

### 4. Cross-Session Search Index

Extend the artifact catalog (when built) to support queries like:
- "Find all hypotheses that reference H2 from session X"
- "Show the evolution of this hypothesis across sessions"
- "List all sessions in research program Y"

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `references` field added to delta schema (delta_output_format_v0.1.md)
- [ ] Reference relation types documented with examples
- [ ] Artifact merge validates reference syntax
- [ ] Lint warns on broken cross-session references (referenced session/item not found)
- [ ] Research program metadata schema defined
- [ ] CLI/web can query cross-session references (depends on artifact catalog)

## Dependencies

- This depends on having an artifact catalog/index (not yet beaded by others)
- Blocking: Nothing directly, but enables research program management

## Non-Goals

- Not building the full catalog search here (that is a separate bead)
- Not building a UI for visualizing hypothesis genealogy (future work)
- Not enforcing references (they remain optional annotations)

## Background: Why Sessions Are Currently Isolated

The current artifact format (artifact_schema_v0.1.md) has `metadata.session_id` but no mechanism for one artifact to reference another. Each session produces a standalone artifact. This was fine for single-session pilots but breaks down when research spans multiple sessions.

The Brenner Method emphasizes building on prior work ("stand on the shoulders of giants") - we need infrastructure that supports this at the session level.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"GoldFalcon","created_at":"2025-12-30T19:00:03.271284703-05:00","updated_at":"2026-01-01T15:00:57.804380303-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-01T15:00:57.804380303-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented cross-session artifact linking: added references field to all item schemas, documented 6 relation types, added Reference/ResearchProgram TypeScript types, implemented reference validation with 12 new tests, and added research_program metadata field to artifact schema.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-prmc","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-jcuz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-30T22:38:17.835895845-05:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"brenner_bot-pts6","title":"TASK: Define Brenner Loop URL Routes & Navigation Structure","description":"
## Where Does the Brenner Loop Live?

The existing site has:

Where does the Brenner Loop go?

### Proposed Route Structure

\`\`\`
/sessions                    → Session list (all user sessions)
/sessions/new                → Start new session flow
/sessions/[id]               → Session dashboard (main view)
/sessions/[id]/hypothesis    → Hypothesis detail/edit
/sessions/[id]/operators     → Operator application workspace
/sessions/[id]/evidence      → Evidence ledger view
/sessions/[id]/agents        → Agent tribunal view
/sessions/[id]/brief         → Research brief / export
\`\`\`

### Navigation Entry Points

From anywhere on the site:
1. **Header CTA**: "Start Research Session" button
2. **Landing page**: Prominent section explaining Brenner Loop
3. **After tutorial**: "Try it yourself" leads to /sessions/new
4. **Operator pages**: "Apply this operator" links to session

### Session List Page (/sessions)

\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Your Research Sessions                    [+ New Session]      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  Active Sessions                                                │

│  │ Started 3 days ago • 2 predictions locked               │   │

│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
\`\`\`

### URL Parameter Conventions

\`\`\`typescript
// Deep linking into session state
/sessions/[id]?phase=refinement    // Jump to phase
/sessions/[id]?operator=level-split // Open specific operator
/sessions/[id]?agent=devils-advocate // Focus on agent panel
\`\`\`

### File Locations

\`\`\`
apps/web/src/app/sessions/page.tsx           → Session list
apps/web/src/app/sessions/new/page.tsx       → New session flow
apps/web/src/app/sessions/[id]/page.tsx      → Session dashboard
apps/web/src/app/sessions/[id]/layout.tsx    → Session chrome
\`\`\`

+ +

+ + + + + +

+

+ +

+

+
+ + {/* Step 1: Research Question */} +
+

+ + 1 + + Choose Your Research Question +

+

+ Good research questions are discriminative: + different answers would lead to different actions (experiments, decisions, models). +

+ +
+
+

Good Example

+

"What determines whether a cell becomes a neuron vs a muscle cell during development?"

+

Different answers → different experiments

+
+
+

Bad Example

+

"What is the meaning of life?"

+

Too vague, no discriminative power

+
+
+
+ + {/* Step 2: Build Excerpt */} +
+

+ + 2 + + Build a Corpus Excerpt +

+

+ Ground your research in Brenner's wisdom. Search the corpus for relevant sections + and compile them into an excerpt file. +

+ + excerpt.md + +# Review the excerpt +cat excerpt.md`} + language="bash" + title="Terminal" + /> + +
+

+ Why excerpts matter: They provide shared + context that all three agents can reference. This reduces hallucination and keeps + agents grounded in the Brenner methodology. +

+
+
+ + {/* Step 3: Write the Kickoff */} +
+

+ + 3 + + Write the Kickoff Prompt +

+

+ The kickoff prompt should include: your research question, the excerpt, and explicit + instructions for productive disagreement. +

+ + +
+ + {/* Save as File */} +
+

+ + 4 + + Save Your Kickoff +

+

+ Save the kickoff to a file. The brenner CLI will read it when starting the session. +

+ + sessions/RS-$(date +%Y%m%d)/kickoff.md << 'EOF' +## Research Question +[Your question here] + +## Context +[Your context here] + +## Relevant Brenner Wisdom +[Paste excerpt.md content] + +## Instructions for This Session +[Your instructions] +EOF`} + language="bash" + title="Terminal" + /> +
+ + + Quality of input → quality of output: The agents can only be as good + as the kickoff you give them. Spend time on this step — a well-crafted kickoff saves + iteration time later. + + + + Include at least one "surprising constraint" in your kickoff — something that + forces agents to think outside their default patterns. For example: "Assume the + most obvious answer is wrong. What would that imply?" + + + {/* Ready Checkpoint */} +
+

+ Kickoff Ready?{" "} + + Save your kickoff file. In the next step, you'll use the brenner CLI to launch + the session and dispatch the kickoff to all three agents. + +

+

+
+ + {/* Step 1: Create Session */} +
+

+ + 1 + + Set Session Variables +

+

+ First, define your session ID. Use a consistent naming convention to track sessions. +

+ + +
+ + {/* Step 2: The Big Command */} +
+

+ + 2 + + Launch the Session +

+

+ This is the main command that initializes everything: +

+ + + +
+

What this command does:

+
    +
  1. + 1 + Registers the Orchestrator agent in Agent Mail +
    2. +
  2. + 2 + Creates a thread with the session ID +
    3. +

+
+

+

+ + 3 + + Verify Messages Were Sent +

+

+ Check that all three agents received their kickoff messages: +

+ + +
+ + {/* Understanding Agent Mail */} +
+

Understanding Agent Mail Threads

+

+ Agent Mail organizes messages into threads. Each session has its own thread ID. +

+ +
+ 
{`Thread: RS-2026-0105-cell-fate
+├── [Orchestrator → BlueLake] KICKOFF: hypothesis_generator role
+├── [Orchestrator → PurpleMountain] KICKOFF: test_designer role
+├── [Orchestrator → GreenValley] KICKOFF: adversarial_critic role
+└── (awaiting agent responses...)`}
+
+
+ + + Session ID is critical: Use the same session ID throughout. It's how + Agent Mail groups related messages and how brenner compiles the final artifact. + + + + You can watch the session in real-time with: brenner session status --thread-id "$SESSION_ID" --watch. + This shows new messages as they arrive. + + + {/* Ready Checkpoint */} +
+

{`┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
+│ Pane 0: Control (you run commands here)     │
+├──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┤

+
+
+ + {/* Step 2: Start Agents */} +
+

+ + 2 + + Start Each Agent +

+

+ In each ntm pane, start the corresponding agent with the session context: +

+ + + +
+

What each agent will do:

+
    +
  1. 1. Register with Agent Mail (if not already)
    2. +
  2. 2. Check inbox for kickoff message
    3. +
  3. 3. Process the kickoff based on their role
    4. +
  4. 4. Post a DELTA message with their contribution
    5. +
  5. 5. Continue iterating based on other agents' responses
    6. +
+
+
+ + {/* Step 3: Monitor Progress */} +
+

+ + 3 + + Monitor the Conversation +

+

+ From your control pane, watch the session progress: +

+ + +
+ + {/* Intervention Patterns */} +
+

When to Intervene

+

+ As the operator, you may need to steer the conversation: +

+ +
+
+

🔄 Agents are looping

+

Send a message asking them to move forward with current hypotheses.

+
+
+

🤝 Too much agreement

+

Ask the adversarial critic to challenge the consensus more aggressively.

+
+
+

❓ Missing third alternative

+

Prompt: "What if both leading hypotheses are wrong?"

+
+
+ + +
+ + + Let them work: Resist the urge to intervene too early. Some apparent + disagreement is productive. Wait at least 10-15 minutes before intervening unless + there's a clear problem. + + + + Take notes on the conversation patterns. Which agent tends to generate the most + creative ideas? Which asks the best critical questions? This informs future + role assignments. + + + {/* Ready Checkpoint */} +
+

+ Session Complete?{" "} + + When all three agents have posted their DELTA messages (usually 20-40 minutes), + you're ready to compile the artifact in the next step. + +

+

+
    +

    • +

    • +

Knowing When to Stop

+

+ Diminishing returns are real. Stop iterating when: +

+ +
+
+ Score plateau: + Scores aren't improving between rounds +
+
+ Circular debate: + Agents are repeating the same arguments +
+
+ Good enough: + The artifact is actionable even if imperfect +
+
+
+ + + Don't over-iterate: A perfect artifact that takes 10 rounds is + often worse than a "good enough" artifact that you can execute on now. + Brenner's philosophy: the best experiment is the one you actually run. + + + + Keep iteration history.
